concept: Spot / Fleck / The Small Round Marking
description: A spot: a small patch of one color resting on another. The fawn's dapple, the egg's speckle, the petal's fleck, the brown-and-white of a patterned coat. 'Piloe' names the mark itself, one unit of the world's spotting, and takes its color as a describer: 'mureli piloe', a brown spot. What wears its spots is patterned by them, and can be known by them long after everything else has changed.
sound_symbolism: 'pi' is the smallest of the stops on the narrowest of the vowels: a dot of sound. 'lo.e' rounds it and sets it down softly, the hiatus holding the spot's edge distinct from its ground, the way the marking sits distinct on the coat.
grammatical_notes: Noun. 'shia whila lo mureli piloe phelu.' (His coat holds brown spots.) Color words stand before it as describers; 'lo piloe' pluralizes the pattern. Kin to 'welisha' (color), whose small local unit it is, and to 'kiroa' (sign), since a spot is often how a thing is known again.

pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: Pattern is the style's native tongue: the dapple, the speckle, the repeated small mark that makes a surface sing.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Herders and foragers know their animals and their eggs spot by spot; the marking is the name the eye uses.
